introSection: Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit is a steel roller coaster currently being demolished at Universal Studios Florida in Orlando, Florida, USA. The ride closed on August 17, 2025. Riders got to choose their own track of music to listen to on the ride. It reached tall and was the first roller coaster to feature a non inverting loop. While on the ride, a video was taken of the riders and the person next to them. They could buy a picture, the video, and an animated e-mail of the ride upon exiting. It was the largest X-Car coaster ever built.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
In March 2008, Universal Studios Florida announced the addition of Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit.

Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit was planned to open in the spring of 2009, but the opening was delayed. The attraction first opened to guests for "technical rehearsals" in the evening of August 15, 2009. It officially opened a few days later on August 19.

The ride received mixed reviews from critics, who praised the unique elements, but criticized the rough ride, theming, the amount of brake runs, and operational issues.

In September 2010, Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit temporarily closed. Maurer had issued an alert warning that stress testing of their X-Car ride vehicles had found that the coupling bars holding the trains together wouldn't be fatigue endurable. The attraction reopened on October 28, 2010 with refurbished trains to improve the ride experience.

For the 2015 season, metal detectors were added to the queue line, along with The Incredible Hulk Coaster and Dragon Challenge as a preventative measure to reduce the likelihood of a guest having any loose articles falling onto the tracks, lift hill mechanisms, other riders, and guests on paths below the tracks.

In early 2017, trim brakes were added to the first drop.

On December 27, 2024, Universal announced that the ride would be closing in September 2025 to make way for a new experience.

On June 2, 2025, the closing date was changed to August 18.

The ride last operated on August 17, 2025. Demolition began over the following days.

On October 10, a section of the ride's lift hill caught fire during demolition.

sections.title: Ride experience
sections.content:
Before entering the queue line, all riders walked through a metal detector. Free lockers were provided for mobile phones and other belongings as they were not permitted on the ride. While queuing, videos explaining how to select a song were shown to riders.

In the station, riders boarded the ride via a moving platform. The platform's speed was synchronized with the trains. Once seated, guests selected a song from a menu featuring a variety of artists using a touchscreen. Riders were also able to select secret songs that required a three-digit code to be entered. The rider did this by holding down the Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit logo for 10 seconds, and then a ten-digit keypad appeared on the screen. Riders then entered the three-digit code, and the secret song played once the ride began. As the train left the station, the music choices were locked. If a rider did not select a song, a random one would play.

Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit began with a vertical lift at a height of. A drop then led into the non inverting loop. A number of twists and four mid-course brakes followed.

At the end of the ride, riders disembarked via another moving platform. At the exit, guests were offered a "music video" consisting of clips of them experiencing the ride that included clips filmed via a front camera and rear facing per seat cameras on each train. The music video did not include any of the three digit secret songs, and a replacement track was instead used.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The ride used X-Car trains which featured large lap bar restraints. Embedded in each restraint was a touch screen which allowed riders to choose the music they wished to listen to on the ride.

sections.title: External links
sections.content:
* BRPH: Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit
 * PCL Construction: Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit

fullText:
Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit is a steel roller coaster currently being demolished at Universal Studios Florida in Orlando, Florida, USA. The ride closed on August 17, 2025. Riders got to choose their own track of music to listen to on the ride. It reached tall and was the first roller coaster to feature a non inverting loop. While on the ride, a video was taken of the riders and the person next to them. They could buy a picture, the video, and an animated e-mail of the ride upon exiting. It was the largest X-Car coaster ever built.

In March 2008, Universal Studios Florida announced the addition of Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit.

Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit was planned to open in the spring of 2009, but the opening was delayed. The attraction first opened to guests for "technical rehearsals" in the evening of August 15, 2009. It officially opened a few days later on August 19.

The ride received mixed reviews from critics, who praised the unique elements, but criticized the rough ride, theming, the amount of brake runs, and operational issues.

In September 2010, Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit temporarily closed. Maurer had issued an alert warning that stress testing of their X-Car ride vehicles had found that the coupling bars holding the trains together wouldn't be fatigue endurable. The attraction reopened on October 28, 2010 with refurbished trains to improve the ride experience.

For the 2015 season, metal detectors were added to the queue line, along with The Incredible Hulk Coaster and Dragon Challenge as a preventative measure to reduce the likelihood of a guest having any loose articles falling onto the tracks, lift hill mechanisms, other riders, and guests on paths below the tracks.

In early 2017, trim brakes were added to the first drop.

On December 27, 2024, Universal announced that the ride would be closing in September 2025 to make way for a new experience.

On June 2, 2025, the closing date was changed to August 18.

The ride last operated on August 17, 2025. Demolition began over the following days.

On October 10, a section of the ride's lift hill caught fire during demolition.



Before entering the queue line, all riders walked through a metal detector. Free lockers were provided for mobile phones and other belongings as they were not permitted on the ride. While queuing, videos explaining how to select a song were shown to riders.

In the station, riders boarded the ride via a moving platform. The platform's speed was synchronized with the trains. Once seated, guests selected a song from a menu featuring a variety of artists using a touchscreen. Riders were also able to select secret songs that required a three-digit code to be entered. The rider did this by holding down the Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit logo for 10 seconds, and then a ten-digit keypad appeared on the screen. Riders then entered the three-digit code, and the secret song played once the ride began. As the train left the station, the music choices were locked. If a rider did not select a song, a random one would play.

Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit began with a vertical lift at a height of. A drop then led into the non inverting loop. A number of twists and four mid-course brakes followed.

At the end of the ride, riders disembarked via another moving platform. At the exit, guests were offered a "music video" consisting of clips of them experiencing the ride that included clips filmed via a front camera and rear facing per seat cameras on each train. The music video did not include any of the three digit secret songs, and a replacement track was instead used.

The ride used X-Car trains which featured large lap bar restraints. Embedded in each restraint was a touch screen which allowed riders to choose the music they wished to listen to on the ride.
